adjectives-1.title: Adjectives I
adjectives-1.content: Adjectives I In Spanish, adjectives must agree in both gender and number with the nouns they describe. Agreement in Gender Adjectives ending in -o change to -a for feminine nouns. el gato negro (the black cat) la gata negra (the black cat (female)) Agreement in Number Adjectives follow the same rules as nouns for making plural forms. los gatos negros (the black cats) las gatas negras (the black cats (female)) Exception! Adjectives that end in -e or a consonant don't change for gender, only for number. el libro grande / la casa grande

adjectives-2.title: Adjectives II
adjectives-2.content: Adjectives II In Spanish, the position of an adjective is just as important as its form. Standard Placement Most adjectives follow the noun they modify, which is the opposite of English. el auto rojo (the red car) English: red car → Spanish: car red Exceptions: Determinative Adjectives Adjectives that indicate quantity or order often precede the noun. muchos libros (many books) la primera vez (the first time) Aesthetic Placement In poetry or literature, adjectives may precede the noun to add emphasis or a descriptive quality, but in daily speech, they almost always follow the noun.

adverbs.title: Adverbs
adverbs.content: Adverbs Adverbs describe how, when, or where an action occurs. Many Spanish adverbs are formed from adjectives. The -mente Ending To form an adverb from an adjective, use the feminine singular form and add -mente (equivalent to -ly in English). Adjective Adverb Rápido (Fast) Rápidamente Lento (Slow) Lentamente Fácil (Easy) Fácilmente Placement Adverbs that modify verbs usually come after the verb. Adverbs that modify adjectives or other adverbs come before the word they modify.

affinity-words.title: Affirmative and Negative Words
affinity-words.content: Affirmative and Negative Words Spanish uses pairs of words to affirm or deny the existence of people or things. Pairings Affirmative Negative Algo (Something) Nada (Nothing) Alguien (Someone) Nadie (No one) Siempre (Always) Nunca (Never) También (Also) Tampoco (Neither) Double Negatives In English, double negatives are avoided. In Spanish, they are required! No veo a nadie. I don't see anyone (I don't see no one).

articles.title: Definite and Indefinite Articles
articles.content: Definite and Indefinite Articles Spanish articles must agree in gender and number with the nouns they modify. Definite Articles (The) Number Masculine Feminine Singular el la Plural los las Indefinite Articles (A, An, Some) Number Masculine Feminine Singular un una Plural unos unas

commands-pronouns.title: Object Pronouns with Commands
commands-pronouns.content: Object Pronouns with Commands The position of object pronouns changes depending on whether a command is affirmative or negative. Affirmative Commands In affirmative commands, pronouns are attached to the end of the verb. Dí melo . (Tell it to me.) Cómpre lo . (Buy it.) Note: Adding a pronoun often requires adding an accent mark to maintain the original stress. Negative Commands In negative commands, pronouns come before the verb (and after 'no'). No me lo diga. (Don't tell it to me.) No lo compre. (Don't buy it.)

comparisons-equality.title: Comparisons of Equality
comparisons-equality.content: Comparisons of Equality To compare things that are equal (as... as), Spanish uses different formulas depending on whether you are using adjectives or nouns. With Adjectives Formula: tan + [adjective] + como Ella es tan inteligente como él. She is as intelligent as him. With Nouns Formula: tanto/a/os/as + [noun] + como Notice that 'tanto' must agree in gender and number with the noun. Tengo tantos libros como tú. (I have as many books as you.) Hay tanta gente como ayer. (There is as much people (as many people) as yesterday.)

comparisons-inequality.title: Comparisons of Inequality
comparisons-inequality.content: Comparisons of Inequality In Spanish, we use specific formulas to compare things that are not equal (more than or less than). More than / Less than más + [adjective/noun] + que (more than) menos + [adjective/noun] + que (less than) Examples Juan es más alto que Pedro. (Juan is taller than Pedro.) Tengo menos dinero que tú. (I have less money than you.) Irregular Comparatives Some adjectives have their own comparative forms: Bueno → Mejor (Better) Malo → Peor (Worse) Viejo → Mayor (Older) Joven → Menor (Younger)

conditional-regular.title: Conditional Tense
conditional-regular.content: Conditional Tense The conditional tense is used to express what would happen. Like the future, endings are added to the infinitive. The Endings Interestingly, the conditional endings are identical to the imperfect endings for -er and -ir verbs. yo -ía tú -ías él/ella -ía nosotros -íamos ellos/ellas -ían Examples Comería (I would eat) Viajaríamos (We would travel) Tendría (I would have - using irreg. stem)

dates.title: Dates
dates.content: Dates Talking about dates in Spanish follows a very specific formula. The Formula To state the date: el + [number] + de + [month] el cinco de mayo (May 5th) el doce de octubre (October 12th) The First Day While we use regular numbers for most dates, for the first day of the month, we use el primero . el primero de enero The Months Months in Spanish are not capitalized unless they start a sentence. enero, febrero, marzo, abril, mayo, junio, julio, agosto, septiembre, octubre, noviembre, diciembre

definite-article-2.title: Definite Article II
definite-article-2.content: Definite Article II In Spanish, the definite article (el, la, los, las) is used in several situations where it is omitted in English. Special Uses With abstract nouns : El amor es importante. (Love is important.) With days of the week : El lunes voy al cine. (On Monday I'm going to the cinema.) With titles (when talking about someone) : El doctor García está aquí. (Dr. Garcia is here.) With body parts and clothing (replaces possessive adjectives): Me lavo las manos. (I wash my hands.)

direct-objects-1.title: Direct Object Pronouns I
direct-objects-1.content: Direct Object Pronouns I Direct object pronouns replace nouns that receive the action of a verb, helping to avoid repetition. The Forms Person Singular Plural 1st me (me) nos (us) 2nd te (you) os (you all) 3rd lo / la (it/him/her) los / las (them) Placement Rule In a simple sentence with one conjugated verb, the direct object pronoun must come before the verb. Yo tengo el libro → Yo lo tengo.

direct-objects-2.title: direct-objects-2
direct-objects-2.content: Direct Object Pronouns II When a sentence contains two verbs (like 'I want to see it'), you have a choice of where to place the pronoun. Placement with Infinitives Option 1: Before the conjugated verb. Lo quiero ver. Option 2: Attached to the end of the infinitive. Quiero ver lo . Both are correct! Spanish speakers use both forms, although attaching it to the end is often considered more natural in spoken Spanish for some learners.

direct-objects-3.title: Direct Object Pronouns III
direct-objects-3.content: Direct Object Pronouns III Just like with infinitives, direct object pronouns can also be attached to present particles (-ando, -iendo). Placement with Particles Option 1: Before the conjugated verb (estar). Lo estoy leyendo. Option 2: Attached to the end of the participle. Estoy leyéndo lo . Accent Alert! Notice that when you attach a pronoun to a present participle, you must add an accent mark to the original stressed vowel of the participle to maintain the correct stress. leyendo → leyéndolo

finale-summary.title: Spanish Finale
finale-summary.content: Spanish Finale: 100 Lessons Milestone Congratulations! You've reached the end of the 100-lesson Spanish Grammar course. What You've Accomplished Over the last 10 units, we have covered: Basics : Gender, Articles, and Regular Verbs. Syntax : Pronoun placement and Sentence structure. The Past : Preterite, Imperfect, and Perfect tenses. The Future : Future and Conditional moods. The Subjunctive : The most complex and beautiful part of Spanish grammar. Keep Practicing! Mastering a language is a lifelong journey. Use these lessons as a reference as you continue to speak, read, and listen to Spanish every day. ¡Buena suerte!

formal-commands.title: Formal Commands
formal-commands.content: Formal Commands Formal commands are used with people you address as 'usted' or 'ustedes' (strangers, superiors, groups). The Formula Step 1: Start with the yo form of the present tense. Step 2: Drop the -o . Step 3: Add the 'opposite' ending. Verb Group Usted Ustedes -ar verbs -e -en -er / -ir verbs -a -an Examples Hablar → yo hablo → Hable / Hablen Comer → yo como → Coma / Coman Escribir → yo escribo → Escriba / Escriban

future-irregular.title: Future Tense: Irregular
future-irregular.content: Future Tense: Irregular Even irregular future verbs use the standard endings. The only thing that changes is the stem of the verb. Common Irregular Stems Tener → tendr- Poner → pondr- Salir → saldr- Venir → vendr- Poder → podr- Saber → sabr- Hacer → har- Decir → dir- Querer → querr- Example: Tener tendré, tendrás, tendrá, tendremos, tendrán Note Irregular verbs in the future will almost always be irregular in the conditional tense as well, using the same stems.

future-regular.title: Future Tense: Regular
future-regular.content: Future Tense: Regular The future tense in Spanish is used to describe what will happen. Unlike most other tenses, the endings are added directly to the entire infinitive . One Set of Endings All regular -ar, -er, and -ir verbs use the same set of endings in the future tense. yo -é tú -ás él/ella -á nosotros -emos ellos/ellas -án Examples Hablaré (I will speak) Comerás (You will eat) Viviremos (We will live) Accent Alert! Notice that all endings EXCEPT the 'nosotros' form have an accent mark.

gender-nouns-1.title: Gender of Nouns I
gender-nouns-1.content: Gender of Nouns I In Spanish, all nouns have a grammatical gender: they are either masculine or feminine. Basic Rules While there are exceptions, the most common way to identify the gender of a noun is by its ending: Masculine Nouns Nouns that end in -o are usually masculine. el chico (the boy) el libro (the book) Feminine Nouns Nouns that end in -a are usually feminine. la chica (the girl) la pluma (the pen) Pro Tip! Always learn the noun with its article (el or la). This is the most reliable way to remember its gender.

gender-nouns-2.title: Gender of Nouns II
gender-nouns-2.content: Gender of Nouns II Beyond -o and -a, there are other common endings that can help you determine a noun's gender. Additional Feminine Endings Nouns ending in these suffixes are almost always feminine: Ending Example Meaning -ción la lección the lesson -tad la libertad the liberty -dad la ciudad the city Common Exceptions: Masculine -ma / -pa Nouns of Greek origin ending in -ma or -pa are usually masculine, even though they end in -a. el problema (the problem) el mapa (the map) el sistema (the system)

gustar.title: Gustar
gustar.content: Gustar The verb gustar doesn't mean 'to like' in the way English does. It literally means 'to be pleasing to.' The Structure The thing being 'liked' is actually the subject of the sentence. Therefore, we mostly use only two forms: gusta (singular) and gustan (plural). Me gusta el café. (I like coffee (Coffee is pleasing to me).) Me gustan los libros. (I like books (Books are pleasing to me).) Indirect Object Pronouns You must use a pronoun with gustar to show who is pleased: me, te, le, nos, os, les ¿Te gusta la música?

hace-ago.title: Hace meaning 'Ago'
hace-ago.content: Hace meaning 'Ago' The verb 'hacer' is used in several time-related expressions, most notably to mean 'ago'. Time Passed Pattern: Hace + Time + que + Present Verb To express how long you have BEEN doing something: Hace dos años que vivo aquí. (I have lived here for two years.) Actions in the Past ('Ago') Pattern: Preterite Verb + hace + Time Fui a España hace tres meses. (I went to Spain three months ago.)

imperfect-irregular.title: Imperfect: Irregular Verbs
imperfect-irregular.content: Imperfect: Irregular Verbs Great news! There are only three irregular verbs in the imperfect tense. Ser, Ir, and Ver Person Ser Ir Ver yo era iba veía tú eras ibas veías él/ella era iba veía nosotros éramos íbamos veíamos ellos eran iban veían Note on Ver Ver is only 'slightly' irregular because it keeps the 'e' from the infinitive (veía instead of vía).

imperfect-regular.title: Imperfect: Regular Verbs
imperfect-regular.content: Imperfect: Regular Verbs The imperfect tense is used to describe ongoing, habitual, or descriptive actions in the past (was doing, used to do). -ar Endings yo -aba tú -abas él/ella -aba nosotros -ábamos ellos/ellas -aban -er / -ir Endings Just like in the preterite, -er and -ir verbs share the same endings in the imperfect. yo -ía tú -ías él/ella -ía nosotros -íamos ellos/ellas -ían Accent Alert! In the imperfect, all -er/-ir endings have an accent mark on the 'i'.

imperfect-subjunctive-1.title: Imperfect Subjunctive I
imperfect-subjunctive-1.content: Imperfect Subjunctive I The imperfect subjunctive is used in the same situations as the present subjunctive (WEIRDO), but when the main verb is in the past tense or conditional. The Formula To form the imperfect subjunctive, you always start with the third-person plural (ellos/ellas) form of the Preterite . Take the 'ellos' preterite form (e.g., hablaron, comieron).Drop the -ron .Add the imperfect subjunctive endings. The Endings yo -ra tú -ras él/ella -ra nosotros -ramos ellos/ellas -ran Accent Rule In the 'nosotros' form, you must add an accent mark to the vowel immediately preceding the ending: habláramos, comiéramos .

imperfect-subjunctive-2.title: Imperfect Subjunctive II
imperfect-subjunctive-2.content: Imperfect Subjunctive II Because the imperfect subjunctive is based on the 'ellos' preterite form, any verb that is irregular in the preterite will have the same irregularity here. Irregular Examples Verb Ellos Preterite Subj. Stem Yo Form Estar estuvieron estuvie- estuviera Hacer hicieron hicie- hiciera Ir/Ser fueron fue- fuera Tener tuvieron tuvie- tuviera Alternate Endings In some regions (especially Spain) and in literature, you may see an alternative set of endings: -se, -ses, -se, -semos, -seis, -sen. Both are correct, but -ra is more common in speech.

imperfect-subjunctive-3.title: Imperfect Subjunctive III
imperfect-subjunctive-3.content: Imperfect Subjunctive III The choice between present and imperfect subjunctive depends on the tense of the main clause. The Sequence of Tenses If the main verb is in Present, Future, or Imperative → Use Present Subjunctive . If the main verb is in Preterite, Imperfect, or Conditional → Use Imperfect Subjunctive . Examples Quiero que vengas. (I want you to come - Present) Quería que vinieras. (I wanted you to come - Past) Polite Requests The imperfect subjunctive of 'querer' (quisiera) is frequently used to make very polite requests. Quisiera un café, por favor.

indirect-objects-1.title: Indirect Object Pronouns I
indirect-objects-1.content: Indirect Object Pronouns I An indirect object tells us to whom or for whom an action is performed. Indirect object pronouns replace these nouns. The Forms Person Singular Plural 1st me (to/for me) nos (to/for us) 2nd te (to/for you) os (to/for you all) 3rd le (to/for him/her/you) les (to/for them/you all) Placement Similar to direct object pronouns, indirect ones usually come immediately before the conjugated verb. Juan me da el libro. Juan gives the book to me.

indirect-objects-2.title: Indirect Object Pronouns II
indirect-objects-2.content: Indirect Object Pronouns II Because le and les can refer to many different people, Spanish often uses extra phrases for clarity. Clarity Phrases Formula: [Pronoun] ... a + [noun/pronoun] Yo le doy el libro a ella . (I give the book to her.) Él les habla a los estudiantes . (He speaks to the students.) Redundancy In Spanish, the indirect object pronoun is often used even when the noun is also present in the sentence. This is perfectly normal and required in many contexts.

indirect-objects-3.title: Indirect Object Pronouns III
indirect-objects-3.content: Indirect Object Pronouns III Just like direct object pronouns, indirect pronouns can also be attached to infinitives and present participles. Attachment Examples With Infinitive: Quiero dar le el regalo. With Participle: Estoy dándo le el regalo. Remember the Choice! You always have the alternative option of placing the pronoun before the conjugated verb: Le quiero dar el regalo.

informal-commands-tu.title: Informal Commands: Tú
informal-commands-tu.content: Informal Commands: Tú Affirmative informal commands are used with friends, family, and people you know well. The Regular Rule For regular verbs, use the third-person singular form of the present tense (the él/ella form). Hablar → Habla (Speak!) Comer → Come (Eat!) Escribir → Escribe (Write!) Irregular Tú Commands There are 8 common irregular commands in the 'tú' form: Decir → di | Hacer → haz | Ir → ve Poner → pon | Salir → sal | Ser → sé Tener → ten | Venir → ven

interrogatives.title: Interrogatives
interrogatives.content: Interrogatives Spanish uses unique punctuation and specific question words to ask questions. Punctuation Rule In Spanish, questions always start with an inverted question mark ( ¿ ) and end with a standard one ( ? ). Common Question Words Spanish English ¿Qué? What? ¿Quién? Who? ¿Cuándo? When? ¿Dónde? Where? ¿Por qué? Why? ¿Cómo? How? Accent Marks Notice that all question words in Spanish have an accent mark when used in a question.

intro-preterite.title: Intro to the Preterite
intro-preterite.content: Intro to the Preterite The preterite tense is used to describe actions that were completed in the past at a specific point in time. When to Use Actions that occurred once. Actions that were part of a chain of events. Actions that occurred within a specific time frame. Keywords If you see these words, you usually need the preterite: ayer (yesterday), anoche (last night), la semana pasada (last week).

irregular-participles.title: Irregular Present Participles
irregular-participles.content: Irregular Present Participles Some verbs have irregular present participles, often due to spelling changes or stem-shifts. The '-yendo' Group When the stem of an -er or -ir verb ends in a vowel, use -yendo instead of -iendo. Leer → leyendo Oír → oyendo Traer → trayendo Stem-Changing Verbs Only -ir stem-changing verbs keep their change in the participle. e → i: Diciendo (from decir) o → u: Durmiendo (from dormir)

irregular-verbs-yo.title: Verbs with Irregular Yo
irregular-verbs-yo.content: Verbs with Irregular Yo Some common Spanish verbs are regular in all forms except for the first-person singular (yo). The 'Go' Verbs These verbs add a g in the yo form. Infinitive Yo Form Meaning hacer hago I do/make poner pongo I put salir salgo I leave traer traigo I bring The 'Yo-oy' Verbs dar → doy ((I give)) ir → voy ((I go))

negation.title: Negation
negation.content: Negation In Spanish, making a sentence negative is much simpler than in English. You just add no before the verb. Rule & Examples Positive: Yo hablo español. Negative: Yo no hablo español. No estoy cansado. (I am not tired.) Ellos no son de aquí. (They are not from here.) Answer with No When answering a question in the negative, you often say no twice: once for the 'No' and once for the sentence negation. —¿Hablas inglés? —No, no hablo inglés.

negative-commands.title: Negative Commands
negative-commands.content: Negative Commands To tell someone NOT to do something, the rules change significantly, especially for the 'tú' form. The Rule for All Forms To form any negative command (Ud., Uds., or tú), start with the formal command form and add 'no' in front. Usted: No hable (Don't speak) Ustedes: No hablen (Don't speak) Negative Tú Commands For the 'tú' form, take the Usted command form and add -s . Hablar → No hable → No hables Comer → No coma → No comas Decir → No diga → No digas

numbers-1.title: Numbers: 1-10
numbers-1.content: Numbers: 1-10 Counting is a basic skill in any language. Let's start with the first ten numbers in Spanish. 1 — Uno 2 — Dos 3 — Tres 4 — Cuatro 5 — Cinco 6 — Seis 7 — Siete 8 — Ocho 9 — Nueve 10 — Diez

numbers-2.title: Numbers: 11-100
numbers-2.content: Numbers: 11-100 As you go higher, Spanish numbers follow a pattern, especially after 20. The Tricky Teens 11 — Once 12 — Doce 13 — Trece 14 — Catorce 15 — Quince Tens and Patterns Number Spanish 20 veinte 30 treinta 40 cuarenta 50 cincuenta 100 cien Building Numbers For numbers between tens, use 'y' (and) for 31 and above: treinta y uno (31). Between 21 and 29, it's one word: veintiuno (21).

passive-voice.title: The Passive Voice
passive-voice.content: The Passive Voice In the passive voice, the subject of the sentence receives the action rather than performing it (The book was written). Option 1: SER + Past Participle This is used when the person performing the action (the agent) is mentioned using 'por'. El libro fue escrito por Cervantes. Note: The participle must agree in gender and number with the subject. Option 2: The Passive 'Se' This is far more common in everyday Spanish when the agent is unknown or unimportant. Se vende pan aquí. Bread is sold here.

past-participles-irreg.title: Irregular Past Participles
past-participles-irreg.content: Irregular Past Participles Many common verbs have irregular past participles that do not follow the -ado/-ido pattern. Common Irregulars Infinitive Past Participle Abrir abierto Decir dicho Escribir escrito Hacer hecho Morir muerto Poner puesto Ver visto Volver vuelto Accent Marks Verbs like leer, oír, and creer require an accent mark on the 'i' to avoid a diphthong: leído, oído, creído .

past-perfect.title: Past Perfect
past-perfect.content: The Past Perfect The past perfect (pluperfect) is used to describe an action that had happened before another action in the past (I had eaten). The Formula Formula: Imperfect tense of HABER + Past Participle Person Haber (Imp.) yo había tú habías él/ella había nosotros habíamos ellos/ellas habían Example Yo ya había comido cuando él llegó. I had already eaten when he arrived.

pero-vs-sino.title: Pero vs Sino
pero-vs-sino.content: Pero vs Sino Spanish has two words for 'but', and they are used in very specific contexts. Pero The standard word for 'but'. It adds information that contrasts with the first part of the sentence. Es caro pero bueno. (It's expensive but good.) Sino Used after a negative statement to mean 'but rather' or 'on the contrary'. No es azul sino verde. It's not blue but (rather) green.

personal-a.title: The Personal 'a'
personal-a.content: The Personal 'a' When the direct object of a verb is a person, Spanish requires a special preposition: the personal 'a' . Usage It doesn't translate to English, but it must be there if the object is human or a pet. Object is a thing: Veo el libro. Object is a person: Veo a Juan. Llamo a mi madre. (I call my mother.) Conozco a esa señora. (I know that lady.)

plural-nouns.title: Plural Forms of Nouns
plural-nouns.content: Plural Forms of Nouns Making nouns plural in Spanish is a straightforward process based on whether the word ends in a vowel or a consonant. Rule 1: Ending in a Vowel If a noun ends in a vowel, simply add -s . el libro → los libros (the books) la pluma → las plumas (the pens) Rule 2: Ending in a Consonant If a noun ends in a consonant, add -es . el borrador → los borradores (the erasers) la ciudad → las ciudades (the cities) Nouns ending in -z If a noun ends in z , change the z to c before adding -es . el lápiz → los lápices

por-para-1.title: Por vs Para I
por-para-1.content: Por vs Para I In English, both 'por' and 'para' are often translated as 'for.' However, in Spanish, they have distinct uses that are never interchangeable. Primary Uses of POR Think of por as expressing movement through a space, duration of time, or cause/reason. Motion or general location (through, along, by, or in the area of) Duration of an action (for, during, in) Reason or motive (because of, on account of, on behalf of) Exchange or substitution (for, in exchange for) Examples of Por Paseamos por el parque. (We walked through the park (Motion).) Estudié por dos horas. (I studied for two hours (Duration).) Gracias por el regalo. (Thank you for the gift (Exchange).)

por-para-2.title: Por vs Para II
por-para-2.content: Por vs Para II While 'por' is about the 'through' or the 'why,' para is about the 'for' in terms of destination, goal, or deadline. Primary Uses of PARA Think of para as expressing a destination, a purpose, or a point in the future. Destination (toward, in the direction of) Deadline or specific time (by, for) Purpose or goal (in order to + infinitive) Recipient (for) Examples of Para Salgo para Madrid. (I'm leaving for Madrid (Destination).) Esta tarea es para mañana. (This homework is for tomorrow (Deadline).) Estudio para aprender. (I study in order to learn (Purpose).) Este regalo es para ti. (This gift is for you (Recipient).)

possessive-pronouns.title: Possessive Pronouns
possessive-pronouns.content: Possessive Pronouns Possessive pronouns are used to show ownership without repeating the noun (mine, yours, theirs). The Forms Possessive pronouns must agree in gender and number with the noun being replaced. Person Masc. Sing. Fem. Sing. Yo mío mía Tú tuyo tuya Él/Ella suyo suya Nosotros nuestro nuestra Using them with Articles In Spanish, you usually include the definite article. Este libro es el mío. (This book is mine.) Tengo mi pluma y la tuya. (I have my pen and yours.)

possessives-1.title: Possessive Adjectives I
possessives-1.content: Possessive Adjectives I Possessive adjectives indicate ownership or relationship. In Spanish, they must agree in number with the noun they modify. Short-Form Possessive Adjectives Person Singular Plural My mi mis Your (inf.) tu tus His/Her/Your (for.) su sus Agreement Rule Possessive adjectives agree with the possessed object , not the possessor. mi libro / mis libros

possessives-2.title: Possessive Adjectives II
possessives-2.content: Possessive Adjectives II For 'our' and 'your' (plural), the possessive adjectives also agree in gender. Nosotros/as & Vosotros/as Adjective Masc. Sing. Fem. Sing. Masc. Plur. Fem. Plur. Our nuestro nuestra nuestros nuestras Your (pl. inf.) vuestro vuestra vuestros vuestras Their / Your (Formal Plural) For 'their' (ellos/ellas) and 'your' (ustedes), we reuse su and sus . su casa / sus libros

present-perfect.title: Present Perfect
present-perfect.content: The Present Perfect The present perfect is used to talk about actions that have happened in the past and continue to be relevant in the present (I have eaten). The Formula Formula: Present tense of HABER + Past Participle Person Haber yo he tú has él/ella ha nosotros hemos ellos/ellas han Forming Regular Past Participles -ar verbs → -ado (hablar → hablado) -er/-ir verbs → -ido (comer → comido / vivir → vivido) He hablado con mi padre. (I have spoken with my father.)

present-progressive.title: Present Progressive
present-progressive.content: Present Progressive The present progressive is used to describe an action that is happening right now . The Formula Formula: Estar + Present Participle (-ando / -iendo) For -ar verbs: replace -ar with -ando (hablar → hablando) For -er/-ir verbs: replace -er/-ir with -iendo (comer → comiendo / vivir → viviendo) Examples Estoy hablando por teléfono. (I am speaking on the phone.) ¿Qué estás comiendo? (What are you eating?)

pret-vs-imp-1.title: Preterite vs Imperfect I
pret-vs-imp-1.content: Preterite vs Imperfect I Deciding between preterite and imperfect is one of the biggest challenges in Spanish. It depends on how you view the action. The Preterite (The 'Box') Use the preterite for actions that are complete or have a clear beginning and end. Specific events List of sequential actions The Imperfect (The 'Line') Use the imperfect for actions that were ongoing , habitual, or descriptive. Characteristics/Descriptions Time, Age, Weather Habitual actions (Used to) Preterite: Comí a las dos. (I ate at 2:00). Imperfect: Comía mucho de niño. (I used to eat a lot as a child).

pret-vs-imp-2.title: Preterite vs Imperfect II
pret-vs-imp-2.content: Preterite vs Imperfect II Often, both tenses appear in the same sentence to describe an action that was in progress when something else happened. Interrupting Actions The imperfect provides the background/setting, and the preterite is the interruption. Yo leía (Imp) cuando Juan entró (Pret). I was reading when Juan entered. Simultaneous Actions If two actions were happening at the same time, use the imperfect for both. Ella cantaba mientras yo bailaba .

pret-vs-imp-3.title: Preterite vs Imperfect III
pret-vs-imp-3.content: Preterite vs Imperfect III Some verbs change their English translation depending on whether you use the preterite or the imperfect. Meaning Changes Verb Imperfect Preterite Saber Knew (info) Found out Conocer Knew (person) Met (for first time) Querer Wanted Tried to Poder Was able to Succeeded/Managed to Tip The preterite often focuses on the start or finish of an action, while the imperfect focuses on the state .

preterite-irregular-1.title: Preterite: Irregular Verbs I
preterite-irregular-1.content: Preterite: Irregular Verbs I Some of the most common verbs in Spanish are completely irregular in the preterite. Interestingly, 'ser' and 'ir' share the exact same forms! Ir & Ser (To go / To be) yo fui tú fuiste él/ella fue nosotros fuimos ellos/ellas fueron Dar (To give) yo di tú diste él/ella dio nosotros dimos ellos/ellas dieron Ver (To see) Ver follows the same pattern as 'dar', but with a 'v'. vi, viste, vio, vimos, vieron

preterite-irregular-2.title: Preterite: Irregular Verbs II
preterite-irregular-2.content: Preterite: Irregular Verbs II Many irregular preterite verbs follow a pattern: they have a special stem and share the same set of endings (which have no accent marks!). Verbs: Estar, Tener, Hacer Estar → estuv- Tener → tuv- Hacer → hic- (hizo in 3rd pers. sing.) The Shared Endings yo -e tú -iste él/ella -o nosotros -imos ellos/ellas -ieron Example: Tener tuve, tuviste, tuvo, tuvimos, tuvieron

preterite-irregular-3.title: Preterite: Irregular Verbs III
preterite-irregular-3.content: Preterite: Irregular Verbs III Continuing with the irregular stems that share the same endings (-e, -iste, -o, -imos, -ieron). Verbs: Poder, Poner, Saber Poder → pud- (to be able to) Poner → pus- (to put) Saber → sup- (to know facts) Example Conjugations Poder: pude, pudiste, pudo, pudimos, pudieron Poner: puse, pusiste, puso, pusimos, pusieron Saber: supe, supiste, supo, supimos, supieron Meaning Changes In the preterite, some of these verbs take on slightly different meanings: Saber (preterite) = to find out Poder (preterite) = to manage to/succeed

preterite-irregular-4.title: Preterite: Irregular Verbs IV
preterite-irregular-4.content: Preterite: Irregular Verbs IV The final group of irregular stems, including those that use a 'j' in their stem and have a slightly different plural ending. Verbs: Querer, Venir, Decir Querer → quis- (to want) Venir → vin- (to come) Decir → dij- (to say/tell) The 'J' Stem Rule For verbs with a 'j' in the stem (like decir), the third-person plural ending is -eron (without the 'i'). Decir: dije, dijiste, dijo, dijimos, dijeron Traer: traje, trajiste, trajo, trajimos, trajeron

preterite-regular.title: Preterite: Regular Verbs
preterite-regular.content: Preterite: Regular Verbs To conjugate regular verbs in the preterite, remove the -ar, -er, or -ir ending and add these forms. -ar Endings yo -é tú -aste él -ó nos. -amos ellos -aron -er / -ir Endings Notice that -er and -ir verbs share the same set of preterite endings! yo -í tú -iste él -ió nos. -imos ellos -ieron

preterite-spelling.title: Preterite: Spelling Changes
preterite-spelling.content: Preterite: Spelling Changes To preserve the original pronunciation of the infinitive, some verbs require a spelling change in the yo form only. The -car, -gar, -zar Rule -car → -qué (buscar → busqué) -gar → -gué (pagar → pagué) -zar → -cé (empezar → empecé) Note These changes only happen in the first-person singular. All other forms follow the regular -ar pattern.

preterite-stem-changers.title: Preterite: Stem-Changers
preterite-stem-changers.content: Preterite: Stem-Changers In the preterite, only -ir verbs that change their stem in the present tense also change in the past. The Rule Stem-changing happens only in the third-person forms (él/ella/usted and ellos/ellas/ustedes). e → i: Pedir (to ask for) o → u: Dormir (to sleep) Conjugation of Pedir (e → i) yo pedí tú pediste él/ella p i dió nosotros pedimos ellos/ellas p i dieron Conjugation of Dormir (o → u) yo dormí tú dormiste él/ella d u rmió nosotros dormimos ellos/ellas d u rmieron

pronouns-combined.title: Combined Object Pronouns
pronouns-combined.content: Combined Object Pronouns When a sentence has both a direct and an indirect object pronoun, follow these two critical rules. Rule 1: Order The indirect object pronoun always comes before the direct object pronoun. Mnemonic: RID (Reflexive, Indirect, Direct). Él me lo da. He gives it to me. (me = indirect, lo = direct) Rule 2: The 'Se' Rule If both pronouns start with an 'l' (le/les + lo/la/los/las), the indirect pronoun changes to se to avoid an awkward sound. Incorrect: Le lo doy. → Correct: Se lo doy.

pronouns-prep-objects.title: Pronouns: Object of Prepositions
pronouns-prep-objects.content: Pronouns: Object of Prepositions When a pronoun follows a preposition (like for, with, about), Spanish uses specific forms that are mostly identical to subject pronouns. The Pronoun Forms Subject Object of Preposition Yo mí Tú ti Él / Ella / Ud. él / ella / ud. Nosotros nosotros Special Cases with 'Con' When used with 'con' (with), 'mí' and 'ti' become: conmigo (with me) | contigo (with you)

reflexive-verbs-1.title: Reflexive Verbs I
reflexive-verbs-1.content: Reflexive Verbs I Reflexive verbs indicate that the subject performs an action on themselves . The Pronouns Reflexive verbs are always accompanied by these pronouns: yo me tú te él/ella se nosotros nos ellos/ellas se Example: Lavarse (To wash oneself) Me lavo las manos. (I wash my hands.) Se lava la cara. (He/She washes their face.) Identifying Reflexives In their infinitive form, reflexive verbs always end in -se (e.g., lavarse, ducharse, levantarse).

reflexive-verbs-2.title: Reflexive Verbs II
reflexive-verbs-2.content: Reflexive Verbs II The placement of reflexive pronouns follows the same rules as direct and indirect object pronouns. Placement Rules Before a conjugated verb : Me levanto temprano. Attached to an infinitive : Quiero levantarme temprano. Attached to a present participle : Estoy levantándome temprano. Emotional Reflexives Many verbs in Spanish are reflexive even if they don't seem like an action performed on oneself in English. Divertirse (To have fun) Aburrirse (To get bored) Enojarse (To get angry)

regular-verbs-1.title: Regular Verbs I (-ar)
regular-verbs-1.content: Regular Verbs I (-ar) In Spanish, verbs are categorized by their endings: -ar, -er, or -ir. This lesson focuses on the most common group: -ar verbs. Conjugation Pattern To conjugate a regular -ar verb in the present tense, remove the -ar to find the stem, then add the following endings: Subject Ending Example (Hablar) Yo -o hablo Tú -as hablas Él / Ella / Ud. -a habla Nosotros/as -amos hablamos Vosotros/as -áis habláis Ellos / Ellas / Uds. -an hablan

regular-verbs-2.title: Regular Verbs II (-er)
regular-verbs-2.content: Regular Verbs II (-er) Regular -er verbs follow a pattern similar to -ar verbs, but using 'e' instead of 'a' in the endings. Conjugation Pattern To conjugate a regular -er verb in the present tense, remove the -er to find the stem, then add the following endings: Subject Ending Example (Comer) Yo -o como Tú -es comes Él / Ella / Ud. -e come Nosotros/as -emos comemos Vosotros/as -éis coméis Ellos / Ellas / Uds. -en comen

regular-verbs-3.title: Regular Verbs III (-ir)
regular-verbs-3.content: Regular Verbs III (-ir) Regular -ir verbs are almost identical to -er verbs in their endings, with exceptions in the 'nosotros' and 'vosotros' forms. Conjugation Pattern To conjugate a regular -ir verb in the present tense, remove the -ir to find the stem, then add the following endings: Subject Ending Example (Vivir) Yo -o vivo Tú -es vives Él / Ella / Ud. -e vive Nosotros/as -imos vivimos Vosotros/as -ís vivís Ellos / Ellas / Uds. -en viven

rel-pronouns-1.title: Relative Pronouns I
rel-pronouns-1.content: Relative Pronouns I: Que Relative pronouns are used to connect two sentences that share a common noun. Que is the most common relative pronoun in Spanish. Using 'Que' Unlike in English, where you can often omit 'that' (the book [that] I read), in Spanish you must always use 'que'. 'Que' can refer to people or things. It translates to: that, which, who, whom. Examples El libro que compré es bueno. (The book (that) I bought is good.) La mujer que habla es mi tía. (The woman who is speaking is my aunt.)

rel-pronouns-2.title: Relative Pronouns II
rel-pronouns-2.content: Relative Pronouns II: Quien While 'que' is the universal pronoun, quien (singular) and quienes (plural) are used specifically for people, usually after a preposition. Using 'Quien' Use 'quien' when referring to people after prepositions like a, con, de, para . La mujer a quien llamaste no está. (The woman whom you called is not here.) Los amigos con quienes viajo son simpáticos. (The friends with whom I travel are nice.) Lo que Use lo que to mean 'what' or 'that which' when referring to an abstract idea, situation, or a previous clause. Lo que necesito es tiempo. (What I need is time).

rel-pronouns-3.title: Relative Pronouns III
rel-pronouns-3.content: Relative Pronouns III For more formal writing or for clarity when the antecedent is far away, Spanish uses compound relative pronouns. El que / El cual These pronouns agree in gender and number with the noun they replace. el que / la que / los que / las que el cual / la cual / los cuales / las cuales Examples They are often used after prepositions of two or more syllables (like cerca de, delante de). La casa en la que vivo es pequeña. The house in which I live is small.

rel-pronouns-4.title: Relative Pronouns IV
rel-pronouns-4.content: Relative Pronouns IV The relative adjective cuyo is used to show possession (whose). Using Cuyo Unlike most pronouns, 'cuyo' must agree in gender and number with the noun that follows it (the thing owned), not the owner. El hombre cuyo hijo es médico... (The man whose son is a doctor...) La mujer cuyas hijas son profesoras... (The woman whose daughters are teachers...)

saber-conocer.title: Saber vs Conocer
saber-conocer.content: Saber vs Conocer Both verbs mean 'to know,' but they are used in completely different contexts. Saber Used for facts, information, and skills (how to do something). Yo sé la verdad. Sé nadar. Conocer Used for people, places, and things (being familiar with). Conozco a María. Conozco Madrid. Conjugation Check Both are irregular in the yo form: saber → sé | conocer → conozco

se-accidental.title: Accidental 'Se'
se-accidental.content: Accidental 'Se' Spanish uses a special construction with 'se' to describe accidental or unintentional events, shifting the blame away from the person. The Construction Pattern: SE + Indirect Object Pronoun + Verb (+ Subject) Se me rompió el vaso. (The glass broke [on me / accidentally].) Se nos olvidaron las llaves. (We forgot the keys [accidentally].) Grammar Logic In this structure, the thing that was dropped/broken is the subject , and the person is merely the indirect object affected by the event.

ser-estar-1.title: Ser vs Estar I
ser-estar-1.content: Ser vs Estar I (Conjugation) In English, the verb 'to be' is always the same. In Spanish, there are two distinct verbs: ser and estar . Conjugation of Ser Subject Ser Yo soy Tú eres Él / Ella / Ud. es Nosotros/as somos Vosotros/as sois Ellos / Ellas / Uds. son Conjugation of Estar Subject Estar Yo estoy Tú estás Él / Ella / Ud. está Nosotros/as estamos Vosotros/as estáis Ellos / Ellas / Uds. están

ser-estar-2.title: Ser vs Estar II
ser-estar-2.content: Ser vs Estar II (Uses of Ser) The verb ser is used to describe permanent or essential qualities. The D.O.C.T.O.R. Acronym Use SER for: D escription (Physical/Personality) O cupation C haracteristic T ime O rigin R elationship Examples Yo soy profesor. (I am a teacher (Occupation).) Ella es de Madrid. (She is from Madrid (Origin).) Hoy es lunes. (Today is Monday (Time).)

ser-estar-3.title: Ser vs Estar III
ser-estar-3.content: Ser vs Estar III (Uses of Estar) The verb estar is used to describe temporary states, moods, and locations. The P.L.A.C.E. Acronym Use ESTAR for: P osition L ocation A ction (Progressive) C ondition (Physical/Mental) E motion Examples Estoy en casa. (I am at home (Location).) Él está cansado. (He is tired (Condition).) Estamos felices. (We are happy (Emotion).)

ser-estar-4.title: Ser vs Estar IV
ser-estar-4.content: Ser vs Estar IV (Comparison) Sometimes, choosing between ser and estar can change the meaning of an adjective. Contrast Table Adjective With Ser With Estar Aburrido To be boring To be bored Listo To be smart To be ready Rico To be wealthy To be delicious Summary Tip Remember: Ser is for WHAT something is, and Estar is for HOW something is at the moment.

si-clauses-1.title: Si Clauses I
si-clauses-1.content: Si Clauses I 'Si' clauses (If clauses) express conditions. There are several types depending on how likely the condition is to be true. Type 1: Possible Conditions If the condition is likely or possible, use the Indicative . Pattern: Si + Present Indicative → Present/Future Indicative Si tengo dinero, iré a España. (If I have money, I will go to Spain.) Note: NEVER use the present subjunctive after 'si' to express a possible condition.

si-clauses-2.title: Si Clauses II
si-clauses-2.content: Si Clauses II Hypothetical or contrary-to-fact conditions require a specific combination of tenses. Type 2: Improbable / Unlikely Conditions If you are talking about something that is not currently true (If I were rich...), use the subjunctive. Pattern: Si + Imperfect Subjunctive → Conditional Si fuera rico, compraría un coche. (If I were rich, I would buy a car.) The Magic Mnemonic A common way to remember this is: Subjunctive-Si, Conditional-Other .

stem-changers-e-i.title: Stem-Changers: e -> i
stem-changers-e-i.content: Stem-Changers: e -> i A smaller group of -ir verbs change the 'e' in the stem to a simple 'i'. Example: Pedir (to ask for/order) Subject Conjugation Yo p i do Él / Ella p i de Nosotros pedimos Ellos p i den Repetir → Repito ((I repeat)) Servir → Sirvo ((I serve))

stem-changers-e-ie.title: Stem-Changers: e -> ie
stem-changers-e-ie.content: Stem-Changers: e -> ie Stem-changing verbs change their spelling in the root of the word for all forms except nosotros and vosotros. Example: Entender (to understand) Subject Conjugation Yo ent ie ndo Tú ent ie ndes Nosotros entendemos Ellos ent ie nden Boot Verbs These are often called 'boot verbs' because if you circle the changing forms on a standard grid, it looks like a boot!

stem-changers-o-ue.title: Stem-Changers: o -> ue
stem-changers-o-ue.content: Stem-Changers: o -> ue Just like e->ie verbs, these verbs change the 'o' in the stem to 'ue' for all forms except nosotros/vosotros. Example: Dormir (to sleep) Subject Conjugation Yo d ue rmo Él / Ella d ue rme Nosotros dormimos Ellos d ue rmen Poder → Puedo ((I can)) Almorzar → Almuerzo ((I eat lunch))

subject-pronouns.title: Subject Pronouns
subject-pronouns.content: Subject Pronouns Subject pronouns are the words that tell us who is performing the action of the verb. Person Singular Plural 1st Person yo (I) nosotros/as (we) 2nd Person (Informal) tú (you) vosotros/as (you all) 3rd Person / Formal él / ella / usted (he/she/you formal) ellos / ellas / ustedes (they/you all formal) Did you know? In many parts of the Spanish-speaking world, ustedes is used for both formal and informal 'you all,' and vosotros is primarily used in Spain.

subjunctive-disish.title: Subjunctive: The Six Irregulars
subjunctive-disish.content: Subjunctive: The Six Irregulars While most subjunctive forms come from the 'yo' form, there are six verbs that are completely irregular. Use the mnemonic DISISH to remember them. DISISH Verbs D ar (dé, des, dé...) I r (vaya, vayas, vaya...) S er (sea, seas, sea...) I r (Wait, the second I is for Ir again or just a mnemonic? Usually it's Dar, Ir, Ser, Haber, Estar, Saber) Let's list them properly below. The Big Six Verb Subj. Form (yo) Dar dé Ir vaya Ser sea Haber haya Estar esté Saber sepa

subjunctive-doubt.title: Subjunctive: Doubt
subjunctive-doubt.content: Subjunctive Usage: Doubt The subjunctive is used to express doubt, denial, or disbelief about a statement. Triggers of Doubt Dudar que (To doubt that) No creer que (To not believe that) No pensar que (To not think that) Es imposible que (It is impossible that) Difference Note the difference between belief and doubt: Creo que (Fact/Belief) → Indicative No creo que (Doubt) → Subjunctive

subjunctive-emotion.title: Subjunctive: Emotion
subjunctive-emotion.content: Subjunctive Usage: Emotion When the main clause expresses a feeling or emotion about an action, the subjunctive is used in the second clause. Emotional Triggers Sentir que (To be sorry that) Alegrarse de que (To be glad that) Temer que (To fear that) Tener miedo de que (To be afraid that) Example Me alegro de que estés aquí. I am glad that you are here. Note Just like with wishes, if there is no change of subject, use the infinitive: Me alegro de estar aquí.

subjunctive-impersonal.title: Subjunctive: Impersonal
subjunctive-impersonal.content: Subjunctive Usage: Impersonal Expressions Impersonal expressions (It is + adjective + that) often trigger the subjunctive, provided they don't express certainty. Triggers Es necesario que (It is necessary that) Es importante que (It is important that) Es posible que (It is possible that) Es una lástima que (It is a pity that) The Exception: Certainty Expressions that imply certainty or truth use the indicative . Es verdad que (It is true that) + indicative Es cierto que (It is certain that) + indicative

subjunctive-intro.title: Subjunctive Intro
subjunctive-intro.content: Subjunctive Intro The subjunctive is NOT a tense; it's a mood . It reflects the speaker's attitude toward an action. Mood vs Tense A tense tells us when something happens (past, present, future). A mood tells us how the speaker feels about it. Indicative : Used for facts and reality (I go, he ate). Subjunctive : Used for hope, doubt, emotion, and uncertainty (I hope he goes). The Trigger Rule For the subjunctive to be used in a dependent clause, you usually need: A main clause with a 'trigger' verb.The connector 'que'.A change of subject.

subjunctive-irregular-yo.title: Subjunctive: Irregular Yo
subjunctive-irregular-yo.content: Subjunctive: Irregular Yo Because the subjunctive is based on the 'yo' form, verbs with irregular 'yo' forms in the present tense carry that irregularity into all subjunctive forms. Verbs with -go, -zco, etc. Tener (yo tengo) → tenga, tengas, tenga... Hacer (yo hago) → haga, hagas, haga... Conocer (yo conozco) → conozca, conozcas... Poner (yo pongo) → ponga, pongas... Why is this important? This rule ensures that the 'hard' or 'soft' sound of the verb is preserved throughout the mood.

subjunctive-ojala.title: Subjunctive: Ojalá
subjunctive-ojala.content: Subjunctive Usage: Ojalá The word Ojalá (meaning 'hopefully' or 'I wish to God') always triggers the subjunctive. Using Ojalá You can use it with or without 'que'. ¡Ojalá llueva! (I hope it rains!) Ojalá que tú ganes. (Hopefully you win.) Origin 'Ojalá' comes from the Arabic 'Insha'Allah', reflecting the long historical influence of Arabic on the Spanish language.

subjunctive-present-regular.title: Subjunctive: Present
subjunctive-present-regular.content: Subjunctive: Present To form the present subjunctive, use the same 'opposite ending' logic as formal commands. The Formula Go to the yo form of the present indicative. Drop the -o . Add the 'opposite' endings. Person -ar verbs -er/-ir verbs yo -e -a tú -es -as él/ella -e -a nosotros -emos -amos ellos -en -an

subjunctive-requests.title: Subjunctive: Requests
subjunctive-requests.content: Subjunctive Usage: Requests Verbs that express a request, command, advice, or suggestion for another person trigger the subjunctive. Request/Influence Triggers Pedir que (To ask that) Decir que (To tell [someone to do something]) Recomendar que (To recommend that) Sugerir que (To suggest that) Example Él me pide que estudie más. He asks me to study more.

subjunctive-spelling-change.title: Subjunctive: Spelling Changes
subjunctive-spelling-change.content: Subjunctive: Spelling Changes Just like with preterite and formal commands, certain verbs require a spelling change in the subjunctive to maintain their sound. The -car, -gar, -zar rule -car → qu (sacar → saque) -gar → gu (pagar → pague) -zar → c (almorzar → almuerce) Note Unlike the preterite (where the change only happens in the 'yo' form), in the subjunctive, the change happened in all forms.

subjunctive-stem-change.title: Subjunctive: Stem-Changers
subjunctive-stem-change.content: Subjunctive: Stem-Changers Stem-changing verbs in the subjunctive follow the same pattern as in the present indicative, with a small exception for -ir verbs. -ar and -er Verbs These verbs change their stem in all forms EXCEPT nosotros (just like the present tense). piense, pienses, piense, pensemos , piensen -ir Verbs These verbs change their stem in all forms, but in the nosotros form, the change is slightly different. e → ie (indicative) becomes e → i in nosotros. o → ue (indicative) becomes o → u in nosotros. Sintamos (from sentir) | Durmamos (from dormir)

subjunctive-wishes.title: Subjunctive: Wishes
subjunctive-wishes.content: Subjunctive Usage: Wishes One of the primary uses of the subjunctive is to express wishes, wants, or desires for someone else. Trigger Verbs These verbs in the main clause trigger the subjunctive in the dependent clause: Querer que (To want that) Desear que (To desire that) Esperar que (To hope that) The Pattern Quiero que tú vengas . (I want you to come.) Note: If there is no change of subject, use the infinitive (Quiero venir).

superlatives.title: Superlatives
superlatives.content: Superlatives Superlatives are used to describe something that has the highest or lowest degree of a quality (the most, the least). The Formula Formula: definite article + [noun] + más/menos + [adjective] + de Juan es el más alto de la clase. (Juan is the tallest in the class.) Es la película menos interesante de todas. (It is the least interesting movie of all.) Absolute Superlatives To say something is 'extremely' or 'very' [adjective], you can add the suffix -ísimo/a/os/as to the adjective. rápido → rapidísimo | inteligente → inteligentísimo

telling-time.title: Telling Time
telling-time.content: Telling Time To tell time in Spanish, we use the verb ser followed by the feminine article to agree with 'las horas'. The Hour Es la una. (It is one o'clock (Singular).) Son las dos. (It is two o'clock (Plural).) Minutes and Fractions Son las tres y diez. (It's 3:10.) Son las cuatro y cuarto. (It's 4:15 (quarter past).) Son las cinco y media. (It's 5:30 (half past).) Son las seis menos cuarto. (It's quarter to six (5:45).)

verbs-like-gustar.title: Verbs like Gustar
verbs-like-gustar.content: Verbs like Gustar Many other verbs in Spanish follow the same 'backwards' structure as gustar. Common Verbs Verb Meaning Fascinar To fascinate Encantar To love/delight Importar To matter/be important Molestar To bother/annoy Quedar To remain/to be left Examples Me encanta el chocolate. (I love chocolate.) ¿Te molesta el ruido? (Does the noise bother you?)
